questions.stem: The four-membered ring highlighted in red is the pharmacologically essential core of this cephalosporin. Resistant bacteria most commonly inactivate the drug by producing an enzyme that hydrolyzes this ring. What are those enzymes called?

explanation.summary: The strained four-membered β-lactam ring is what covalently binds and inhibits transpeptidases. Bacteria resist β-lactam antibiotics chiefly by secreting β-lactamases, enzymes that cleave the amide bond of this ring and render the drug inactive.
explanation.whyCorrect: The strained four-membered β-lactam ring is what covalently binds and inhibits transpeptidases. Bacteria resist β-lactam antibiotics chiefly by secreting β-lactamases, enzymes that cleave the amide bond of this ring and render the drug inactive.

questions.stem: This schematic depicts the layered bacterial envelope in which alternating sugar subunits are joined into a rigid meshwork that protects the cell from osmotic lysis. Formation of which bonds within this structure is the step blocked by cephalosporins?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hydrogen bonds stabilizing the lipid bilayer

choices.label: C
choices.text: Glycosidic bonds between the sugar backbone

choices.label: D
choices.text: Phosphodiester bonds in the plasma membrane

explanation.summary: The rigidity of the peptidoglycan wall comes from transpeptidase (penicillin-binding protein) enzymes cross-linking adjacent peptide chains that dangle from the NAM-NAG sugar backbone. Cephalosporins are structural analogs of the D-Ala-D-Ala substrate and covalently inhibit these transpeptidases, halting cross-linking so the growing cell lyses.
explanation.whyCorrect: The rigidity of the peptidoglycan wall comes from transpeptidase (penicillin-binding protein) enzymes cross-linking adjacent peptide chains that dangle from the NAM-NAG sugar backbone. Cephalosporins are structural analogs of the D-Ala-D-Ala substrate and covalently inhibit these transpeptidases, halting cross-linking so the growing cell lyses.
